theory.intro: A unit fraction has a numerator of $1$. You can decide whether it is smaller or larger than $\tfrac{1}{2}$ by looking at its denominator.
facts: $\tfrac{1}{2}$ splits one whole into **two** equal parts.
A bigger denominator means **more, smaller** parts — so the part is **less** than $\tfrac{1}{2}$.
$\tfrac{1}{2}$ is the **only** unit fraction equal to a half; $\tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{4}, \dots$ are all smaller.
steps: Look at the denominator of the unit fraction.
If the denominator is more than $2$, the fraction is **less** than $\tfrac{1}{2}$.
Compare to $\tfrac{1}{2}$: $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is the largest unit fraction except $1$ itself.

foundation.q: Which unit fraction is exactly equal to $\tfrac{1}{2}$?
A pizza is cut into $4$ equal slices. Is one slice more or less than half the pizza?

development.q: A glass is $\tfrac{1}{4}$ full. Is it more or less than half full, and by how many quarters?
How many fifths make up a half? Is $\tfrac{1}{5}$ more or less than $\tfrac{1}{2}$?
Is $\tfrac{1}{2}$ the largest possible unit fraction? Explain.
Compare $\tfrac{1}{6}$ and $\tfrac{1}{2}$ using a sixths diagram.
A chocolate bar has $10$ equal pieces. Is $1$ piece more or less than half?
Which is larger, $\tfrac{1}{3}$ or $\tfrac{1}{2}$, and how many more thirds reach a half?
Of the unit fractions $\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{4}, \tfrac{1}{8}$, which is half of the one before it?

mastery.q: Without a diagram, explain why $\tfrac{1}{7}$ is less than $\tfrac{1}{2}$.
mastery.a: Splitting into $7$ parts gives smaller parts than splitting into $2$

mastery.q: A jug is $\tfrac{1}{3}$ full and needs to be at least half full. What fraction more is needed (in sixths)?
Two pizzas the same size: one cut in $3$, one in $5$. You take one slice of each. Which slice is bigger and is either bigger than half?
mastery.a: The thirds slice is bigger; neither is bigger than half

mastery.q: Mia ate $\tfrac{1}{4}$ of a cake; Noah ate $\tfrac{1}{3}$ of the same cake. Together did they eat more or less than half?
A unit fraction is just less than $\tfrac{1}{2}$ but bigger than $\tfrac{1}{4}$. Which is it?
True or false: every unit fraction with denominator $3$ or more is less than $\tfrac{1}{2}$.
A water bottle is $\tfrac{1}{5}$ full. How many more fifths to pass the halfway mark?
Explain why $\tfrac{1}{2}$ shaded covers more of a shape than $\tfrac{1}{6}$ shaded, even on the same shape.
Choose the unit fraction nearest to $\tfrac{1}{2}$: $\tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{4}, \tfrac{1}{2}$ excluded. Which is nearest?
